item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Image has been taken down. Per Wikimedia Commons,

No authorship information, unknown copyright situation. Probably not own work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This wasn't doing anything for me until I got to the part of the mushrooms speaking French. I hate French so the idea of some giant French mushrooms screaming in agony while they burned to death made me smile.

Upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Got a few links to propose here. First off, the mention of everyone's favorite Hazmat based MTF can be turned into a link to their spot on the MTF hub. Secondly, it seems to me that the SCPF may be tempted to test SCP-2381 instances using SCP-3536, as it allows them to literally blow the bacteria up in size for study. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

In the event of an outbreak, MTFs Chi-7 ("Plague Tamers") and Beta-7 ("Maz Hatters") are to be deployed and establish standard quarantine procedures.

To this:

In the event of an outbreak, MTFs Chi-7 ("Plague Tamers") and Beta-7 ("Maz Hatters") are to be deployed and establish standard quarantine procedures.

Adding this to the end of the Containment Procedures:

Use of SCP-3536 for study of SCP-2381 samples has been tentatively approved, pending fulfillment of appropriate BSL requirements.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: Darkest Dungeon Fans?
item.content:encoded: As a fan of Darkest Dungeon, I couldn't help but be reminded of this fucked up thing. Did you get inspiration from it? Or just a coincidence? Either way, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this article, going from "oh, ok, a kinda aggressive strain of S. cerevisiæ" to "woah, what is that really?!".

+1

(Also there's a minor typo: "behavior similar to that of Candida alibcans.")

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually like this. It might be a little biased, but penis-based body horror genuinely scares the crap out of me.

However, I have to agree with some of the other commenters; the angry shrooms' radio transmissions are actually funny, which counters the horror aspect.

On another note:

Information regarding SCP-2381 causes a reaction in human males similar to the memetic effect of SCP-5972-J. Further investigation may be required to determine a possible link between SCP-2381-2 and The Kirby Company.

~Dr. Shrooms

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The footnotes in this article are just weird. A lot of it is over analyzing and restating things, stating things that are unnecessary to know, or stating things which would already be known to readers in universe.
To be more clear, I said Keter, Yeast, Forest in the chat for a brainstorming game and he thought of the idea that eventually turned into this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed the issues that both you and TL333s pointed out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a no vote for me as it stands. The length is a bit of an issue, and the body horror is kinda meh. I was doing to downvote initially, but there's something about the radio conversations I find kind of endearing, and I feel like it would be a good thread to something I'd throw at my players in an RP I used to run.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair point although it's more like a, "Dick Measuring" contest, in a literal and metaphorical sense, than trying to get revenge, and Humans are just caught in the crossfire.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are quite a few grammatical errors:

+ show
- hide

Should a treatment be discovered; these procedures will be reviewed and modified as needed.

Comma, not semicolon.

sodium-chloride

Not a compound word, so no hyphen.

Non-foundation personnel aware of the anomaly are to be treated with Class-B amnestics.

Capitalize Foundation.

Attempts to directly communicate and dissuade SCP-2381-2 from causing future outbreaks are being researched, however; should such attempts fail, the O5 council has approved several neutralization plans.

Switch the comma and semicolon. The independent clause ends at "researched," and the comma should go after "however." (There are several of these sentences throughout.)

The hyphae strands do this by drilling into the host cells plasma membrane, penetrating small out branches into the interior.

Host cells' (possessive) and I think you mean "small out-branches," implying that the branches branch out, but it's still a little awkwardly structured.

These structures, hereby dubbed SCP-2381-1, emit aerial spores at a constant frequency consistent with most multi-cellular fungi, however; the fruiting body never rots and continues releasing spores for an indeterminate duration.

Semicolon's in the wrong place. The clause ends after "fungi" so the semicolon goes there, and the comma goes after "however."

including vomiting, blood laced coughs

Blood-laced (compound adjective).

Near the end of stage 4, infected individuals possessing a penis and testes

Nothing wrong with this, just wanted to say thank you for saying "people with penises" instead of "men" because it's so effing easy to do in clinical writing and yet I rarely see it happen on this site

developing infantile spore producing structures.

Spore-producing (another compound adjective).

This process is similar in individuals possessing a functional vagina-cervix-uterus, however the clitoral glans is forced to merge with the urethral and vaginal openings, as the fruiting body develops.

Technically okay, but I think a semicolon after "-uterus" and comma after however would flow better clause-wise.

undergo internal review before the ethics committee for her failure to properly contain the anomaly

Ethics Committee would be capitalized (proper noun).

Operatives complained of random interference, sudden and sometimes painful high pitched tones

High-pitched.

Investigation of the malfunctioning equipment away from the quarantine zone concluded that the transceivers were functioning correctly, however; when returned to the mobile task force units, the previously described issues resumed.

Another backwards semicolon and comma. Semicolon after "correctly," comma after "however."

Transmissions, consisted of a series of extended high pitch tones occurring at 30 minute intervals

Extraneous comma, and it'd be high-pitch. (Or high-pitched.)

Inquiries by lingual and radio transmission experts were able to discern a series of communications, in french

Capitalize French.

Survey teams from Chi-7 were unable to locate the source of the transmission on the surface, however; biological analysis of the vineyard owned by "patient zero" discovered a number of rotting fruiting bodies next to several notably large specimens of Ash and Oak trees.

Again, the semicolon goes after the first clause, which ends at "surface." Comma after "however."

Concept-wise, no-vote. It drags on for quite a while, but I'm not getting too much out of the concept other than surface-level body horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think this is more like Keter dick shrooms. Close, but not quite.

I like the body horror, but the "mushrooms trying to get revenge on trees" narrative is so goofy in juxtaposition. The final addendum just really threw me out of the whole thing, so a no-vote from me.

I'd recommend moving the paragraph about the radio signals to after the information about the infection stages. It follows a little more logically, plus it's weird to define -2 before -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You made your bed. Now lie in the bed of keter dicks, you monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After a bunch of discussion in chat, we came to the horrifying realization that I had written Keter Dicks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Minor grammar quibbles
- Hide
  • In the last paragraph of containment procedures: "Media referring to or depicting an outbreak is to be intercepted and altered. Non-foundation personnel aware of the anomaly are to be treated with Class-B amnestics." Having this as one sentence is iffy (it reads like "altered non-foundation personnel").
  • Foonote 8 should read "Whose vineyard".
  • Stage 5's first sentence should not contain a colon. It can read "Stage 5 mostly concerns the development…"; you can also omit the comma before the "and" in that sentence.
  • Capitalize "French" in the paragraph before the transmission log. I think changing "Unknown" to "SCP-2381-2" would help make the transmissions read clearer. If you think it's not explicit enough, you could change "an unknown entity dubbed SCP-2381-2" to "a heretofore unknown entity (later dubbed SCP-2381-2)". You refer to it as -2 later, so I don't think changing it in the log will make a huge difference.

My beef with this skip, and most that get to this length, is that phrases and sentences that seem to restate themselves make the article longer than it needs to be.

"Samples are to be cultured using YEPD medium" has no significance to anyone with no experience in mycology, and even within the field is likely a distinction that wouldn't merit listing in the procedures any more than the chemicals used in the following sterilization process would be.

The last paragraph feels a little heavy handed and may only be necessary because the length of the article makes it easy to skim through. If you can trim down on the containment procedures where possible I think you could remove the last paragraph and have the article remain punchy.

No vote for now; I like the idea and I think that the depiction of the infection deserves the length and detail it was given, but I think parts of the containment could be cut shorter to help the article keep from dragging during the introduction.

EDIT: Changed my vote to +1, due to changes and in no small part because one of the teams was called "Maz Hatters".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've removed the majority of the aforementioned paragraph, and planted a few more subtle indirect hints as to what's going on in other places in the article. Hopefully this improves it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't mean for it to come off that way, it was a suggestion made to me, however if it actively detracts I will consider removing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The paragraph actually does detract from the article and that's a little condescending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Normally I would agree with you, but it was suggested that I add that last paragraph as a sort of "Bludgeon" to ensure people get what's really going on. I'd like to think I've made it pretty clear what's going on, but sometimes people really need it just plainly pointed out to get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this enough to upvote, but I think it would really improve this if you ditched the last paragraph of the addendum: It's just recapping stuff that's already been demonstrated in a more interesting, indirect way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Overly detailed description (the 'stages' section in particular doesn't seem necessary), a tired 'origin' story that doesn't add anything interesting (it's almost 100% bland exposition), -2 could be better established. Nothing here jumps out as unique or interesting. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like some parts of this. I always enjoy thorough and detailed containment procedures, but I agree with ARD that this feels really long-winded in places. We don't even get to the main effect until we're a significant way through the article, and the five-stage progression is kind of awkward and stiff. I would recommend taking some pruning shears to the description and try to cut out everything you don't need. Brevity is the soul of wit, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not feeling this one. Most of the body horror is buried under collapsibles necessitated by long-winded pedantic regurgitations of mycology class, and the narrative pitch at the end is a stylistic retread of SCP-1291.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it already a picture in use? Well wasn't aware of that. I'll find something else.

I went ahead and removed it. I'll find another picture to use later on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't that picture in a SCP too? It was made by Dr. Solo?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey Guys and gals, here is my fourth SCP, and my 5th work published to the site. Many thanks go out to Bluemorpho for the idea inspiration, ARD for the narrative pitch, Weryllium, AbsentmindedNihilist, Thetataur, and Levi for their critiques and reviews. I hope you enjoy this squicky body horror adventure.

Image is licenced under creative commons and was posted to Wikimedia commons. https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Levedurasvivas.jpg

Edit: Heavy thanks to Roget for his feedback on the narrative stuff.
